insights.insight: Verified purchase receipts settle at roughly $0.10 per confirmed conversion event versus $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic ad tech attribution — a 50–200x cost delta that exists because the receipt eliminates the attribution problem rather than modeling around it.

Chrome's deprecation of third-party cookies removes the last major cross-site tracking primitive, and no current alternative — fingerprinting, first-party data, or data brokers — produces identity-free, cryptographically verified purchase signals across ecosystems; this protocol does.

insights.topic: Physical retail dominance in the dataset
insights.insight: King Soopers, Kroger, Walmart, Costco, Target, Publix, Home Depot, and Dollar General now dominate the verified receipt corpus, confirming the camera-based OCR pipeline is capturing real-world brick-and-mortar commerce at scale — the harder and more commercially valuable problem.

Amazon sees Amazon purchases, Walmart sees Walmart purchases, banks see transaction amounts but not SKUs — no single platform has cross-retailer basket-level data; 3,827 verified receipts across these brands in one aggregate layer is a structural first.

Active receipt data is now coming from rural and non-metro states including Kentucky, Maine, Wisconsin, and Tennessee, meaning the dataset is not a coastal tech-demo sample — it is beginning to reflect the geographic distribution of actual U.S. consumer spending.

insights.topic: Platform silo vulnerability for brand measurement
insights.insight: Brands running campaigns across Amazon, Walmart, and physical grocery cannot currently reconcile purchase attribution across those channels in a single, identity-free signal layer; $224,753 in verified GMV across these retailers in one protocol suggests that reconciliation is now technically possible.
